preamble: In Semitic languages rich in pharyngeal consonants, the low vowel [a] undergoes pharyngealization or retraction when adjacent to pharyngeal and guttural consonants like [ʕ] and [ħ]. The vowel is backed to [ɑ] or [ɒ], and secondary articulation increases pharyngeal constriction and tension. This is a common coarticulatory effect across Arabic dialects and Biblical Hebrew, where pharyngeal consonants trigger vowel quality changes including lowering, backing, and emphasis spread.

examples.context: Backed allophone before pharyngeal consonants [ʕ] and [ħ]
Pharyngealized (emphasized) allophone in emphatic consonant context
Vowel harmony: emphasis from pharyngeal consonants spreads across entire word, including distant syllables
Lowering of schwa to [a] after guttural consonants (א, ה, ח, ע)
